evidence_quotes.quote: Likewise, the rule must permanently eliminate token reactivation loopholes whereby a company briefly relaunches or patches a software to reset a regulatory clock and maintain monopoly control.

Once you announce stopping security updates for your program or operating system or neglegt them, you have voluntarily left it behind, and you should not retain legal power to stop the community from preserving or reviving it.
This forfeiture must apply to the latest functional state of the product, or as close to it as technically possible.

paragraphs.text: I support the creation of a permanent use-it-or-lose-it rule within the Digital Fairness Act to protect consumers, small developers, and Europe's digital cultural heritage. When a company abandons a program or operating system meaning it announces the end of support, shuts down essential servers, or stops further development of security updates so that customers can no longer update what they bought for at least twelve months that company should be deemed to have permanently forfeited its exclusive rights to that product. This has to include operating systems as well. A purchasable version of any operating systems or programs must be offered, and security updates in particular must not generate any additional costs. Once you announce stopping security updates for your program or operating system or neglegt them, you have voluntarily left it behind, and you should not retain legal power to stop the community from preserving or reviving it. Ownership ends when stewardship ends. This forfeiture must apply to the latest functional state of the product, or as close to it as technically possible. Companies must not be allowed to evade responsibility by releasing a crippled or token compliance build that barely runs but satisfies paperwork. The preserved or community-maintained version should reflect the final, working build that consumers actually experienced before shutdown. Likewise, the rule must permanently eliminate token reactivation loopholes whereby a company briefly relaunches or patches a software to reset a regulatory clock and maintain monopoly control. Under a true use-it-or-lose-it model, exclusive rights cannot be revived later; the community remains free to operate servers, patch clients, and maintain access for users who already paid. Crucially, the solution must avoid fines, license fees, and regulatory costs that only large publishers can absorb. Any fixed penalty whether simply strengthens monopolies and destroys smaller creators who cannot afford compliance lawyers or perpetual maintenance. Requiring ongoing updates or lifelong support likewise turns passion projects into liabilities. The goal is fairness through deregulation, not new financial hurdles. Under use-it-or-lose-it, the rule is self-executing: maintain a minimal playable build and keep your rights; abandon it and the public may preserve it. No fees, no bureaucracy, no distortion of competition. This approach protects independent creators who generally welcome preservation while removing the leverage that large corporations use to suppress modders, archivists, historians, and community servers. It guarantees that software remain accessible in their authentic and functioning form and that the market rewards stewardship rather than deletion. In economic terms, it restores value consumers already purchased and prevents digital destruction of property. In political terms, it is proportionate and clear: if a business wants to keep control, it can simply keep the software up-to-date and save; if it abandons the product for twelve months, exclusivity ends permanently. Stopping security updates reduces exclusivity to 28 days after the last functioning update. I therefore urge the Commission to include a Permanent Use-It-or-Lose-It Abandonment Forfeiture Clause for software within the Digital Fairness legislative package. This is a fair, efficient, non-bureaucratic solution that defends consumers, protects creators, and preserves investements aligned with the goals of a fair, open, and sustainable digital single market.
